title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

texts.A: The fashionable contemporary concept of 'sustainable fashion' is, upon any serious examination, a fundamental contradiction in terms and a deeply misleading proposition. The fashion industry's entire business model is structurally and deliberately predicated on the engineered principle of planned obsolescence—the constant and systematic creation of new artificial 'needs' and the rapid psychological obsolescence of perfectly functional garments. [38]Merely substituting conventional cotton crops with certified organic alternatives or sourcing recycled synthetic fibers does absolutely nothing to address the core and catastrophic problem: the sheer, staggering volume of waste produced by a consumer culture that has normalized treating clothing as an entirely disposable and trivially cheap commodity.[/38] [39]Genuine, meaningful change will only materialise when we collectively and decisively stop purchasing 'new' altogether and commit to a genuinely radical cultural transformation centred on repair, reuse, and vibrant second-hand exchange.[/39] [37]Everything short of this fundamental transformation is merely sophisticated 'greenwashing' deployed by corporations to soothe the guilty conscience of the environmentally anxious consumer.[/37]
texts.B: While I broadly share the deep and legitimate skepticism regarding the sincerity and depth of corporate sustainability commitments, we must simultaneously and honestly recognise the undeniable transformative power of 'incremental progress' when it is applied consistently and at truly global scale. Perfect should not be allowed to become the enemy of measurably and significantly better. [37]If every major global retailer simultaneously committed to transitioning their entire supply chain to recycled polyester, closed-loop water treatment systems, and verified ethical labour practices, the cumulative environmental impact on pollution, water consumption, and carbon emissions would be genuinely and substantially massive.[/37] Furthermore, and this point is frequently overlooked, the global fashion and textile industry directly and indirectly provides meaningful employment to many millions of workers in developing economies who have no comparable alternative livelihood. A total consumer boycott, however morally satisfying to its advocates, would constitute an economic catastrophe for the very vulnerable workers we rhetorically claim to be protecting. [40]We need to fundamentally reform the supply chain from within, not attempt to destroy an industry that supports livelihoods.[/40]
texts.C: [38]I agree that the sheer and relentless volume of garment production represents the most acute and immediate crisis in global fashion, but I disagree that expanding second-hand consumer markets constitutes anything close to a complete or genuinely sustainable solution to a systemic problem.[/38] The inconvenient reality is that significant quantities of donated second-hand clothing are simply baled and exported to markets in the Global South, where the flood of cheap foreign garments systematically and devastatingly undermines and destroys pre-existing local textile industries and artisanal traditions. I broadly share the view that we must as a matter of priority redirect our efforts toward designing and valuing genuine 'durability' over seasonal novelty. [40]The most promising structural innovation is a transition toward a 'rental' model for high-end fashion and a 'subscription' model for everyday basics, in which the manufacturing brand retains legal ownership and ongoing responsibility for the garment throughout its entire useful life and at its end-of-life disposal.[/40]
texts.D: The extensive policy and media debate over 'sustainable materials' and 'circular waste systems' consistently and frustratingly overlooks what I believe is actually the single most powerful and immediately implementable lever for driving genuine and lasting change in consumer behaviour: radical and comprehensive supply chain transparency. If every single garment sold were legally required to carry a verified 'digital passport' providing real-time data on its full lifecycle carbon footprint, the precise wages and working conditions of every worker involved in its production, and its complete material composition, genuinely conscious consumers would naturally and powerfully gravitate toward ethical choices without requiring additional regulation. While I fully share the view that the ongoing 'fast fashion' phenomenon is an environmental and social disaster of considerable magnitude, I also believe the market is already demonstrating a meaningful generational shift in values. [39]Generation Z demonstrates a notably stronger and more authentic interest in 'vintage' aesthetics, 'upcycling' creative practices, and individual style over brand loyalty than any consumer generation that has preceded it.[/39]

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the effectiveness of corporate sustainability initiatives?
questions.explanation: Expert A calls corporate efforts 'greenwashing' and ineffective. Expert B argues for 'incremental progress' and claims the impact of retailers switching to better materials would be 'massive.'

questions.question: Which expert shares A's opinion about the fundamental problem being the volume of production?
questions.explanation: Expert A focuses on the 'sheer volume of waste' and the culture of new 'needs.' Expert C explicitly agrees that 'the volume of production is the real crisis.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to D regarding the changing habits of younger consumers?
questions.explanation: Expert D mentions Gen Z's interest in 'vintage' and 'upcycling.' Expert A also points toward a 'culture of repair and second-hand exchange' as the only way forward, aligning with those values.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the best way to handle the future of the fashion industry?
questions.explanation: Expert B wants to 'reform the supply chain' within the current model. Expert C proposes a 'radical' move to a 'rental' or 'subscription' model where ownership is fundamentally changed.
